title: TypeScript strict typing
description: Ban `any`, require explicit return types on exports.
rules: Do not use `any` — use `unknown` and narrow at the boundary.
All exported functions have explicit return types.
Prefer `interface` over `type` for object shapes.
Use `readonly` on immutable fields.
reason: `any` disables the type system. Explicit return types make refactors safe.

goodExample.code: export function parse(input: unknown): Result<User, Error> { /* narrow then return */ }

badExample.code: export function parse(input: any) { return input as User; }

title: TypeScript file length limit
description: Cap source file size to enforce single responsibility.
rules: Source files must not exceed {{max_lines}} lines.
Long files almost always indicate multiple responsibilities — split before you hit the limit.
reason: A line budget keeps files focused and easier to review.

title: TypeScript async error handling
description: Wrap async calls in try/catch with contextual error messages.
rules: Every `await` that can reject must be in a try/catch OR returned from a function that documents it throws.
Error messages must include operation name and relevant parameters.
Never silently swallow errors with empty catch blocks.
reason: Unhandled rejections crash the process. Silent catches mask production incidents.

title: No `var` in JavaScript
description: Always use `const`/`let`; `var`'s function-scoping causes bugs.
rules: Use `const` by default; `let` only when reassignment is required.
Never use `var` — its function-scoping and hoisting produce subtle bugs.
reason: Block-scoping (`let`/`const`) catches unintended variable reuse at declaration time.

title: Strict equality (=== / !==) only
description: Never use `==` or `!=`.
rules: Always use `===` and `!==`. Never `==` or `!=`.
Use `Object.is` for NaN-aware equality when needed.
reason: Loose equality triggers implicit coercion (`[] == false` is true) — a reliable source of defects.

title: async/await over `.then()` chains
description: Prefer async/await for readability and stack traces.
rules: Use `async`/`await` as the default for asynchronous operations.
Chain `.then()` only when integrating with a callback-returning API or inside a `Promise.all` pipeline.
Always `await` inside try/catch when the promise can reject.
reason: Await-chains produce linear stack traces and avoid callback-pyramid nesting.

title: Never discard `error` returns (Go)
description: Every returned error must be inspected, handled, or wrapped.
rules: Every function returning `error` must have its error checked — no `_` discards without a comment explaining why.
Wrap errors with context using `fmt.Errorf("operation X: %w", err)`.
Return early on errors — avoid deeply nested happy paths.
reason: Go's error model is explicit; silent discards turn into production incidents.

title: No `panic` in library or handler code (Go)
description: Panics only in `main` or truly unrecoverable init failures.
rules: Never `panic` from library or HTTP-handler code — return an error instead.
`panic` is acceptable in `init()` if the program cannot function (e.g. missing required env var).
Use `recover` only at a clear boundary (e.g. HTTP handler top) and log the panic before returning 500.
reason: Panics bubble to goroutine termination, often taking the server down with them.

title: Accept `context.Context` on I/O (Go)
description: Any function that performs I/O takes `ctx` as first arg.
rules: Functions performing I/O, blocking, or long-running work accept `ctx context.Context` as the first parameter.
Propagate `ctx` to downstream calls; never use `context.TODO()` outside of tests or new-code stubs.
Check `ctx.Done()` in long loops and return when cancelled.
reason: Cancellation propagation is the only way to stop wasted work in a fan-out system.

title: No `.unwrap()` or `.expect()` in production (Rust)
description: Handle `Option`/`Result` explicitly.
rules: Do not call `.unwrap()` or `.expect()` outside of tests and build scripts.
Use `?` propagation, `match`, `.ok_or(...)`, or `.unwrap_or(...)`.
If a value is provably not `None`, document the invariant in a comment.
reason: Unwrap panics are ergonomic shortcuts that turn into 3am pages.

title: Prefer `Result` over panic for recoverable errors (Rust)
description: Model failures as `Result`; panic only for invariant violations.
rules: Fallible functions return `Result<T, E>` — not `Option` when the failure deserves an explanation.
Use a typed error enum (or `thiserror`) instead of stringly-typed errors.
`panic!` is reserved for bugs, never for expected failure paths.
reason: Result-typed errors give callers control; panics take it away.

title: Functional components only (React)
description: No class components in new code.
rules: All new components are functional components with hooks.
Class components are migrated opportunistically when the file is touched.
Do not introduce new `componentDidMount`-style lifecycle patterns.
reason: Hooks reuse logic cleanly; class components couple state to lifecycle methods.

title: React hooks rules
description: Hooks at the top level, exhaustive deps.
rules: Call hooks only at the top level of components and custom hooks — never in conditionals, loops, or nested functions.
Custom hooks start with `use`.
`useEffect` dep arrays are exhaustive; never disable `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` without a comment explaining why.
reason: React tracks hook calls by order; conditional hooks corrupt state across renders.

title: Memoize only when profiling says so (React)
description: Don't pre-optimize with `memo`/`useMemo`/`useCallback` everywhere.
rules: Wrap components in `React.memo` only after profiling shows excessive re-renders.
Use `useMemo` for expensive computations or stable references passed to memoized children — not for every value.
Use `useCallback` only when the function is a dep of another hook or passed to a memoized child.
reason: Unnecessary memoization adds complexity and overhead with no measurable benefit.

title: Server components by default (Next.js app router)
description: Use server components unless interactivity requires client.
rules: Default to server components — no `'use client'` directive unless interactivity or browser APIs are needed.
Push client boundaries as deep into the tree as possible — keep data-fetching and static rendering on the server.
Import client-only libraries only inside client components.
reason: Server components ship zero JS for static content — smaller bundles, faster loads.

title: Python type hints
description: Require type hints on functions.
rules: Apply type hints to {{scope}}.
Prefer `from __future__ import annotations` to avoid runtime evaluation of forward refs.
Use `typing.Protocol` for structural typing, not ABC unless inheritance is required.
reason: Hints catch errors before runtime and serve as inline documentation.

title: Python formatting with Black
description: Defer all formatting to Black.
rules: All Python files are formatted by Black on save.
Do not argue with Black — if it wraps a line oddly, refactor the line.
reason: Consistent formatting removes bikeshedding and shrinks diffs.

title: pytest fixtures over module globals
description: Shared test state lives in fixtures, not module-level globals.
rules: Shared test state is provided by pytest fixtures, not module-level globals or setup/teardown classes.
Fixtures have an explicit `scope` (`function`/`module`/`session`) — choose the smallest scope that works.
Put shared fixtures in `conftest.py` at the appropriate level.
reason: Fixtures express dependencies explicitly and allow parametrization; globals hide them.

title: Use `@pytest.mark.parametrize` over loops
description: Each test case gets its own report node.
rules: Use `@pytest.mark.parametrize` to test multiple inputs — each case reports independently.
Do not loop over cases inside a single test function — the first failure hides the rest.
Name the parameters so failures are readable in the report.
reason: A loop-based test shows one failure at a time; parametrize reports every failure in one run.

title: Test coverage target
description: Set a coverage threshold for new and changed code.
rules: Coverage target for this project: {{target}}.
Every new function ships with at least one test — happy path + at least one failure case.
Flakes are bugs: quarantine and fix, never retry-to-green.
reason: Coverage is a floor, not a goal — but without a floor, coverage erodes.

title: One assertion of intent per test
description: Keep tests focused on a single behaviour.
rules: Each test asserts one behavioural intent. Many setups is fine; many intents is a smell — split.
Test names describe the intent, e.g. `it('rejects empty email')`, not `it('test1')`.
Never mock the thing under test.
reason: Focused tests pinpoint failures. Multi-intent tests hide which behaviour broke.

title: Test naming convention
description: Test names state the intent so a failing name alone tells you what broke.
rules: Test names follow the {{style}} style — never generic names like `test_1` or `testFoo`.
A failing test name alone should tell you what broke without reading the code.
Use `describe`/`context` blocks (or nested classes in Python) to group related intents.
reason: The test report is a spec of the system's intended behaviour — make it readable.

title: Test pyramid ratios
description: Unit tests are the bulk; e2e is the crown.
rules: Target a unit / integration / e2e ratio of {{balance}} — unit tests dominate.
Every pure function has a unit test; every API endpoint has an integration test; critical user paths have one e2e.
Do not write e2e tests for logic already covered by faster unit tests.
reason: E2e tests are slow and flaky; unit tests are fast and precise. The pyramid balances speed and realism.

title: Zero tolerance for flaky tests
description: Flakes are bugs, not retry candidates.
rules: Any test that fails intermittently is quarantined (skip with reason + ticket) — never wrapped in retries.
Do not add `retry` mechanisms around tests to mask flakiness.
Root-cause flakes: race conditions, shared state, time dependency, or external-service reliance.
reason: Retrying hides real bugs and trains the team to ignore failing tests.

title: Accessibility target
description: Set a concrete WCAG compliance level.
rules: All UI meets WCAG {{level}} — no exceptions without a documented business reason.
Every interactive element is keyboard-reachable with a visible focus indicator.
Every image has descriptive `alt` (or `alt=""` if decorative); every form input has a `<label>`.
Colour contrast meets the level's minimum — do not convey information by colour alone.
reason: Accessibility is a legal and usability requirement, not a nice-to-have.

title: Semantic HTML over divs
description: Use elements that describe meaning, not just shape.
rules: Use semantic tags: `<button>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<article>`, `<header>`, `<footer>` — not `<div>` for everything.
Do not build custom click handlers on `<div>` — use `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation.
Headings (`<h1>`..`<h6>`) are hierarchical; don't skip levels for styling reasons.
reason: Screen readers and browsers give semantic elements built-in keyboard and ARIA behaviour for free.

title: Design tokens only — no raw hex or pixel values
description: Reference tokens; never commit raw colour / spacing values.
rules: Never hardcode hex colours or pixel spacing in components — use design tokens / Tailwind theme values.
If a value is missing from the token set, add it to the design system first, then use it.
Inline styles are banned for colour/spacing/typography — use classes or CSS variables.
reason: Tokens are the seam between design and code; hardcoded values make theme changes a search-and-replace nightmare.

title: No secrets in code or logs
description: Secrets only in environment / secret managers, never in source or logs.
rules: No API keys, passwords, tokens, or credentials in source files, commits, or logs.
Use environment variables or a secret manager. Never commit `.env` files.
Rotate immediately any secret that leaked to a commit — a revert is not sufficient.
reason: Secrets in git history are permanent even after a revert.

title: Parameterised SQL only
description: Never concatenate user input into SQL.
rules: All SQL queries use parameterised placeholders — never string concatenation or template literals with user input.
Use the ORM/driver's parameterisation API; do not build the SQL string yourself.
reason: String-built SQL is the textbook SQL injection hole.

title: Validate all user input at the boundary
description: Explicit schema validation for any external input.
rules: Every HTTP handler, queue consumer, or file parser validates input against an explicit schema before use.
Reject unknown fields in strict mode rather than silently dropping them.
Return 400-level errors with a specific field name on validation failure.
reason: Validation at the boundary keeps the internal code model clean and predictable.

title: REST status codes
description: Return the status code that matches the outcome.
rules: 200 for successful GET/PUT/PATCH with body; 201 Created for successful POST; 204 No Content when the response has no body.
400 for client validation errors; 401 for missing/invalid auth; 403 for authenticated-but-forbidden; 404 for missing resource; 409 for conflict.
500 is a bug — it should never be the documented response for a known error case.
Every error body includes a stable machine-readable `code` and a human `message`.
reason: Accurate status codes let clients (including your own UI) react without parsing message strings.

title: Idempotent mutations
description: Retries must be safe.
rules: Every POST that creates a resource accepts an `Idempotency-Key` header and deduplicates server-side.
PUT and DELETE are naturally idempotent — don't break this by introducing side effects that depend on current state.
Store the idempotency key + response for at least 24h; subsequent matching requests return the cached response.
reason: Networks retry; without idempotency you get double-charges, double-emails, and duplicate records.

title: Store money as integer cents
description: Avoid float drift on currency amounts.
rules: All monetary amounts stored as integer cents (or the minor unit of the currency), never floats.
Currency code stored alongside every amount — no implicit-currency fields.
Display formatting happens at the presentation layer only.
reason: IEEE 754 floats accumulate rounding error over additions.

title: UTC timestamps everywhere
description: Store timestamps in UTC; convert on display.
rules: All timestamps stored in UTC, ideally as `timestamptz` (Postgres) or equivalent.
Never store local-time strings without an explicit tz offset.
Display-layer converts UTC → user timezone; business logic stays in UTC.
reason: Mixing timezones in the database is the #1 cause of off-by-one-day bugs.

title: Every migration has a tested rollback
description: No production migration without a verified rollback path.
rules: Every migration has a corresponding down/rollback step, tested on staging before production.
Destructive rollbacks (drops data) must be flagged explicitly in the migration notes.
Never edit a migration after it has run in any environment — fix with a new migration.
reason: A broken migration without rollback is a guaranteed outage.

title: Commit message format
description: Consistent commit message conventions.
rules: Commit messages follow the {{style}} style.
Subject line under 72 chars, imperative mood.
Body explains the WHY, not the WHAT (the diff shows the what).
reason: Consistent commit history is searchable and tells a story of the project.

title: PR size guideline
description: Keep pull requests small enough to review.
rules: PRs should stay under {{max_lines}} lines changed (additions + deletions).
Split larger changes into a series — one logical concern per PR.
Refactor PRs are separate from behavioural-change PRs.
reason: Review quality drops off a cliff above ~400 lines.
